fixtures.description: need=1 and b is present (1 >= 1), so the requirement is satisfied and the result is empty
need=2 but only c is present (1 < 2), so missing keys are listed; result compared via string coercion
options is the string "abcde": json-logic-js checks it as a single (missing) key but uses its character length (5) in the threshold math, so 5 - 1 = 4 meets need=4 and the result is empty
same string "abcde" but need=5 exceeds 5 - 1 = 4, so the single whole-string key "abcde" is returned (compared via string coercion)
json-logic-js uses options.length (UTF-16 code units), so the emoji is one missing key but contributes length 2; 2 - 1 = 1 meets need=1 and the result is empty
need=0 means any number of these is fine, so the result is always empty
path strings flow through the same dot-walker as missing
json-logic-js spreads the options string as an array-like arg list (missing.apply), so "a" becomes the single key "a"; it is missing, so the result is ["a"] (compared via string coercion)
threshold uses raw JS >=, so >= NaN is never satisfied and the missing keys are returned; NaN is produced via division-by-zero since JSON has no NaN literal
+Infinity clamps to Int.max so the requirement can never be met
a non-numeric string threshold coerces to NaN, so >= is never satisfied and all keys are returned; result compared via string coercion
